questions.scenario: You need to know how many files are in /home/student. Counting by eye isn't an option in production.
questions.question: Count the number of files and directories in /home/student using a pipe.
questions.description: The pipe | sends stdout of one command directly into stdin of the next. ls | wc -l counts lines of output. This is the foundation of the Unix philosophy: small tools chained together.
hints: ls lists files, wc -l counts lines
Connect them with a pipe character |

questions.scenario: You want to create a directory and immediately move into it. Both must succeed — if mkdir fails, don't cd.
questions.question: Create /tmp/workdir and change into it, ensuring the second command only runs if the first succeeds.
questions.description: && (AND) runs the right side only if the left side exits 0 (success). || (OR) runs the right side only on failure. These are fundamental for safe command chaining in scripts.
use_cases: Stop a deploy if tests fail
Only backup if the source exists
hints: You need && between the two commands

questions.scenario: You need to know which log levels appear most often in /home/student/logs/app.log — ERROR, WARN, or INFO.
questions.question: Show a frequency count of log levels, highest first, using the frequency idiom on /home/student/logs/app.log.
questions.description: sort | uniq -c | sort -rn is the frequency idiom. First sort groups identical lines together, uniq -c counts consecutive duplicates, then sort -rn ranks by count descending. You'll use this daily.
use_cases: Rank most active users in access logs
hints: Extract the level field first with grep -oE '(INFO|WARN|ERROR)'
Then pipe into: sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

questions.scenario: A monitoring script needs to display disk usage AND write it to a log file at the same time.
questions.question: Run 'df -h /' and send output to both the terminal and /tmp/disk.log simultaneously.
questions.description: tee reads from stdin and writes to both stdout AND a file simultaneously. It's essential when you need to see output in real-time while also saving it.
use_cases: Log script output while watching it live
Save command output while displaying it in a pipeline
Duplicate a stream for dual consumption
hints: The command is tee, placed at the end of a pipe
tee takes a filename as argument

questions.scenario: You have a list of process names in /home/student/data/processes.txt and need to pass each one as an argument to echo (simulating a command per line).
questions.question: Use xargs to run 'echo PROCESS:' before each line from /home/student/data/processes.txt.
questions.description: xargs reads lines from stdin and passes them as arguments to a command. It bridges commands that produce output with commands that need arguments — not all commands accept stdin.
use_cases: Run a command on each file found by find
Apply an operation to each result from grep
hints: Pipe the file contents into xargs
xargs passes each line as an argument to the command
